investor.name: Paul Graham
investor.about: Essayist, programmer, and co-founder of Y Combinator. Created Viaweb (acquired by Yahoo for $49M), then founded YC in 2005 which has funded over 5,000 startups worth over $600B combined. Known for essays on startups, programming languages, and technology.

company.name: VC Whisper
company.description: AI-powered investor research tool for founders. Enter an investor name or paste a pitch transcript, get a full briefing with career history, portfolio, relevant tweets, game plan, and predicted questions.

fitScore.explanation: PG has backed multiple developer tools and founder-facing SaaS products through YC. VC Whisper's thesis (founders waste time on bad-fit investors) maps directly to his 'do things that don't scale' philosophy, and the product itself is the kind of unsexy-but-essential tool he writes about. The concern is that investor tooling is a crowded category and PG typically backs products with broader market potential.
fitScore.thesisAlignment: Strong. PG values tools that remove friction from the startup process. VC Whisper eliminates hours of pre-meeting research.
fitScore.portfolioPatternMatch: Moderate. YC has backed founder tools (Clerky, Stripe Atlas) but VC Whisper is narrower in TAM than PG typically prefers.
fitScore.stageFit: Perfect. Pre-seed with traction is PG's sweet spot.
fitScore.checkSizeFit: $1.5M raise fits within YC's standard deal range.

career.note: Founded the most influential startup accelerator. Funded Stripe, Airbnb, Dropbox, Coinbase, DoorDash, Reddit, and 5,000+ others. Combined portfolio value over $600B.
Built one of the first web application platforms for e-commerce. Acquired by Yahoo for $49M. Became Yahoo Store.
200+ essays on startups, programming, and technology. 'Do Things That Don't Scale', 'Schlep Blindness', and 'How to Start a Startup' are canonical founder reading.

press.title: Paul Graham on the Future of Startups
press.summary: Two-hour conversation covering AI startups, founder qualities PG looks for, and why he still writes essays. Said the best founders 'notice things that are broken and can't stop thinking about fixing them.'

press.title: The Best Essay on Startups I've Read This Year
press.summary: New essay on why the best startup ideas look like bad ideas at first. Referenced customs brokerage as an example of 'schlep blindness.'

relevantContent.text: The most undervalued startup ideas are tools that help other startups. Everyone wants to build for consumers, but the founders who build for founders understand the pain viscerally.
relevantContent.relevance: VC Whisper is exactly this: a tool built by founders (Parth and Tyler) for other founders. PG's own essay argues this category is undervalued.

relevantContent.text: The best startup ideas come from noticing something broken that you personally experience. Not from brainstorming sessions.
relevantContent.relevance: Parth and Tyler built VC Whisper because they experienced the pain of under-prepared investor meetings themselves. Classic PG origin story.

relevantContent.text: If you can get 62,000 people to watch your launch in 24 hours, you've found something people want. That's the only signal that matters at the seed stage.
relevantContent.relevance: VC Whisper's launch numbers (62K views, 174 likes, 20 retweets) are exactly the kind of organic traction PG values.

recentTweets.text: The most common mistake founders make is to solve problems no one has. The next most common is to try to make money before making something people actually want.
Schlep blindness is the single biggest source of overlooked startup ideas. People avoid the hard, boring stuff, and that's where the money is.
The best startup ideas look like bad ideas at first. That's exactly the kind of thing that turns into a $10B company.

gamePlan.howTheyThink: Paul Graham evaluates in the first 60 seconds. He fires rapid questions to understand what you actually do, not what you think you do. He values conciseness over polish: if you can't explain VC Whisper in one sentence, he'll assume you don't understand your own product. He gets excited about tools that remove friction from processes everyone accepts as painful, and he probes hard on 'why now' and 'why you.' He famously hates slides. Just have a conversation.

connections.title: You built a tool for founders
connections.detail: PG has written repeatedly that founder tools are undervalued. VC Whisper is a tool built by founders who experienced the pain of unprepared investor meetings. This is the exact origin story PG looks for.

connections.title: 62K organic launch
connections.detail: PG's primary signal at pre-seed is organic demand. 62K people watched the launch tweet. 174 likes. 20 retweets. No paid marketing. This is 'making something people want' in its purest form.

connections.title: The stack is the moat
connections.detail: VC Whisper combines Exa, Seedlist, xAI, and Browserbase into a single workflow that would take a founder hours to replicate manually. The integration layer is the product. PG values this kind of 'boring but deep' technical work.

connections.title: Expand beyond prep
connections.detail: VC Whisper started with pitch analysis (the original 62K launch), then added Prepare (pre-meeting research). The trajectory is clear: full fundraising lifecycle. PG loves wedge strategies that start narrow and expand.

connections.title: AI-native, not AI-wrapped
connections.detail: VC Whisper isn't a ChatGPT wrapper. It orchestrates multiple AI services (Exa for search, xAI for tweets, Browserbase for scraping) with domain-specific prompt engineering. PG distinguishes between 'uses AI' and 'is AI.'

predictedQuestions.question: What do you actually do?
predictedQuestions.suggestedApproach: One sentence: 'Founders enter an investor's name, and we generate a complete briefing: their career, portfolio, relevant tweets, and a game plan for the meeting.' Then shut up and let him ask follow-ups.

predictedQuestions.question: Why would someone pay for this when they can just Google the investor?
predictedQuestions.suggestedApproach: Because Googling takes 2 hours and misses the connections. VC Whisper cross-references their portfolio, recent tweets, and your specific company in 60 seconds. Show the Paul Graham example output. The depth speaks for itself.

predictedQuestions.question: What's the bigger play here?
predictedQuestions.suggestedApproach: Fundraising is broken. Founders waste months meeting wrong-fit investors. We started with meeting prep because it's the sharpest pain point. Next: investor discovery (who should you even be talking to), then CRM, then the full fundraising stack. Every founder raises money; the TAM is every startup.

predictedQuestions.question: How do you make money?
predictedQuestions.suggestedApproach: Freemium. Free tier with basic briefings. Pro tier ($49/mo) with API-enriched data, PDF exports, unlimited briefings. Enterprise tier for accelerators and law firms who do this for their portfolio companies.

avoid.reason: PG has been publicly skeptical of startups that lead with 'AI' as a differentiator. Lead with the problem (founders are unprepared for investor meetings) and the result (a complete briefing in 60 seconds). Let him discover the AI is sophisticated, don't pitch it.

avoid.topic: Don't compare to LinkedIn Sales Navigator or Crunchbase
avoid.reason: PG thinks in terms of novel categories, not competitive positioning against incumbents. VC Whisper isn't a better database; it's a new workflow. Comparing to existing tools makes it feel incremental.

otherInvestors.name: Jared Friedman
otherInvestors.why: Current YC partner. Deeply technical. Has been vocal about developer tools and founder infrastructure. Would understand the API orchestration architecture immediately.
otherInvestors.talkingPoint: Show him the tech stack: Exa + Seedlist + xAI + Browserbase orchestrated into a single query. He'll appreciate the engineering.

otherInvestors.name: Elad Gil
otherInvestors.why: Prolific angel investor in developer and founder tools. Wrote 'High Growth Handbook.' Understands the fundraising process deeply from both sides.
otherInvestors.talkingPoint: His own book advocates thorough investor research. VC Whisper automates the process he recommends.

otherInvestors.name: Sarah Tavel
otherInvestors.why: Framework-driven investor (Hierarchy of Engagement). Would evaluate VC Whisper through engagement depth: do founders come back before every meeting?
otherInvestors.talkingPoint: Repeat usage data. If founders use it before every meeting, that's the 'core action' she looks for.

otherInvestors.name: Josh Wolfe
otherInvestors.why: Backs unsexy infrastructure. Vocal about information asymmetry. VC Whisper reduces information asymmetry between founders and investors.
otherInvestors.talkingPoint: Frame it as an information asymmetry play. Investors have databases; founders have Google. VC Whisper levels the field.

quickReference.oneLinerThesis: Tools for founders are undervalued. Remove friction from painful processes.
quickReference.oneLinerStyle: 60-second evaluation. Rapid questions. Hates slides. Wants conversations.
topQuestions: Why would someone pay vs. Googling?
quickReference.doNotSay: Don't lead with 'AI-powered.' Don't compare to Crunchbase.
